title: Add framing_style parameter to task dispatch prompts
description: The reading note argues that notation choices aren't neutral — they activate different problem-solving modes and invite different communities. Applied to Mira: natural language task descriptions aren't neutral specifications, they prime the agent's solution topology. The improvement: add a `framing_style` field to task descriptions dispatched to sub-agents, with two variants — 'procedural' (step-by-step: 'First do X, then Y, then Z') and 'constraint' (outcome-based: 'Output must satisfy: X, Y, Z'). This lets Mira run controlled comparisons on the same underlying task to test whether framing style systematically shifts the solution structure the agent generates.
rationale: The note's core claim: 'same task described procedurally vs as constraints — does the agent's solution topology differ systematically?' This is a direct testable hypothesis about Mira's own prompt engineering. The current architecture dispatches tasks as flat natural language strings with no explicit framing awareness. Adding framing_style makes the implicit choice explicit and enables controlled A/B testing across task types — exactly the experiment the note proposes.
diff_description: In prompts.py: define a FramingStyle enum (PROCEDURAL, CONSTRAINT). Add a build_task_prompt(task, framing_style=FramingStyle.PROCEDURAL) function that wraps the core task description in either a step-sequence scaffold ('Step 1: ... Step 2: ...') or a constraint scaffold ('Requirements: ... Constraints: ... Output criteria: ...'). In writer.py and general.py: thread framing_style through the task dispatch call, defaulting to PROCEDURAL for open-ended creative tasks and CONSTRAINT for structured/analytical tasks. Log the framing_style used alongside task output so Mira can compare solution structures in post-hoc analysis.
